titleStmt.title: Letter from Marie Martin to her mother Mary Lewis Martin, 20 August 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Marie Martin

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Marie Martin (1892-1975) to her mother Mary Lewis Martin (née Moore). In this letter Marie talks to her mother of her workload, comprising of fifty-six gas poisoning cases. She mentions visiting a wounded friend, Jack Leonard (b.1890), and hearing from another, G. Gartland, who is in the trenches. Marie asks after her brother Tommy(b.1891) and references the surprising nature of events in Greece.Marie was the founder of the Medical Missionaries of Mary. At the outbreak of war in 1914 Marie joined the Voluntary Aid Detachment, a division of the Red Cross. She returned home briefly in 1916 following the death of her brother in battle until being called to serve again two months later in a field hospital in Heraldo, France.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
20-8-16. My very fondest Mother. Just a very hurried
lines to say all is well with me
I have had a much busier week
than I expected when last writing
as I had to open & look
after 56 Stretcher Gas Poisoning
cases for four days with only
myself & one orderly but have got
help now & shall write & tell
you all about it to-morrow.
Glad to say every thing went
fairly well & now I have
Miss Panel to help me & we can
set our 3 hours off each day.
I hope this will find you
all very well at home, many
thanks for your letter I
do not see much chance of a half
day very soon but ifI get
the chance will certainly go
& see Jack Leonard. I am
sorry to hear he is wounded
but I hope not badly. I
had a letter from G. Garttan
on Thursday written on the 30th
of July but made a mistake
in my address & put 38 instead
of 25th so it has been all over
France. He is back in the
Trenches again & he says his
nerves are very bad. I wish he
could get a rest for a bit.
How is Tommy these times
the affairs in Greece are
rather surprising. Must say
good-night a big rest &
